research.title: Passkeys in Next.js: Killing the Password with WebAuthn
research.whyTrending: Passkeys hit mainstream adoption in 2025-2026; ties directly to the new auth pillar (NextAuth/Clerk) but the WebAuthn implementation itself is uncovered and highly searched.

seo.suggestedTitle: Passkeys in Next.js: Passwordless Auth with WebAuthn

gaps: Tutorials show the two round-trips but skip WHY (challenge/ceremony model)
rpID footgun (must match domain, changing it invalidates all passkeys) underexplained
Server-side verification gotchas (unique challenge, strict origin, sign counter) glossed
Little on the DB schema you must store (credentialID, publicKey, counter, transports)
Username-less / discoverable credential UX rarely shown end to end
strengths: SimpleWebAuthn is the de facto library

officialSources.note: rpID must match domain; not on Public Suffix List

expertInsights: Public-key crypto: private key never leaves device; server stores public key; server sends challenge, device signs, server verifies
Two ceremonies, two round-trips each: registration (options -> attestation) and authentication (options -> assertion)
rpID must match your domain exactly; changing the URL/rpID invalidates ALL existing passkeys; rpID cannot be on Public Suffix List
Server MUST generate a fresh unique challenge per request and validate origin + clientData strictly
DB must store: credentialID, public key, sign counter, transports per user
Sign counter detects cloned authenticators; must be persisted and checked
Discoverable (resident) credentials enable username-less login via the browser account chooser
SimpleWebAuthn ~4.9M weekly downloads mid-2026 - de facto standard
Cross-device/hybrid flows more fragile than same-device
commonProblems: Changing rpID/domain and bricking every users passkey
Reusing challenges (replay) or lax origin validation
Storing publicKey/credentialID in the wrong encoding

strategy.angle: A build-it guide that leads with the mental model (challenge-response, two ceremonies) so the two round-trips make sense, uses SimpleWebAuthn, and spends real space on the production footguns nobody warns about - rpID invalidation, unique challenges, strict origin, the sign counter, and the exact DB schema - then wires it into the existing auth setup as a passwordless option
strategy.title: Passkeys in Next.js: Killing the Password with WebAuthn
strategy.excerpt: Passkeys replace passwords with a private key that never leaves the users device. Implementing them in Next.js is two ceremonies and four round-trips - and a handful of production footguns (rpID invalidation, challenge reuse, the sign counter) that silently break auth. Heres the build and the traps.
hooks: Contrarian: passkeys are simpler than passwords once you stop thinking in passwords and start thinking in challenge-response
Footgun: change your domain and you invalidate every passkey your users ever created
Practical: SimpleWebAuthn does the crypto; the bugs are all in your server verification and DB
outline: Hook: the login with no password field
The mental model: challenge, sign, verify
The two ceremonies and what crosses the wire
The database schema you must store
Footgun 1: rpID and the domain trap
Footgun 2: unique challenges and strict origin
Fitting passkeys alongside your existing auth
